note_en:
(CNN)The mysterious head injuries suffered by US diplomatic staff in China and Cuba that had been  described as "sonic attacks" are consistent with the use of directed microwave energy, according to a report published Saturday by the  National Academy of Sciences.
 "Overall, directed pulsed RF (radio frequency) energy, especially in those with the distinct early manifestations, appears to be the most plausible mechanism in explaining these cases among those that the committee considered," the report said.
 The report added that it considered possible incidents of persistent postural-perceptual dizziness as a possible secondary issue in some of those affected a contributing factor.
    While the report does not conclude that the use of directed microwave energy in these instances was done deliberately, it said such action could be used for nefarious purposes.
 "The mere consideration of such a scenario raises grave concerns about a world with disinhibited malevolent actors and new tools for causing harm to others, as if the U.S. government does not have its hands full already with naturally occurring threats," the report said.
  The report said that it considered chemical exposures, infectious diseases and psychological issues as potential causes or aggravating factors of the injuries, but the overall analysis appeared to show they were not the likely cause.
 A 19-person committee of medical and scientific experts  produced the report at the request of the State Department.
 CNN  reported last year that doctors shared what happened to the brain of one diplomat who may have been the victim of one of the attacks.
  Researchers revealed the results of an independent brain analysis of Mark Lenzi, a US diplomat who was stationed in Guangzhou, China, in 2017 when he started experiencing unexplained symptoms, including headache, difficulty reading, irritability, as well as memory and sleep problems.
 Among the MRI findings: 20 brain regions with "abnormally low" volumes, including regions involved in memory, emotional regulation and motor skills that may correlate with Lenzi's symptoms, doctors said. Of the 107 regions they looked at, they also found three that had bigger volumes.
 Researchers said the parts of the brain with low volume may reflect brain injury, and those with high volumes could be evidence that other parts of his brain have compensated.
 Most of the documented attacks came in 2016 and 2017, though there were a handful of reported incidents after that as well.
 CNN  reported in 2018 that a senior administration official said investigators tore apart buildings where diplomatic employees encountered the sounds but found no acoustic devices. That lead law enforcement to believe the injuries were the result of microwaves beamed from a nearby location and that the "sounds" were merely a means of masking the microwave attacks.
 That was only a theory, the official said, and there was no concrete evidence to back it. However, brain scans on the injured personnel showed changes that indicate damage, the official said.
  Cuban officials have consistently denied their government had any involvement with the diplomats' health problems and have said mass hysteria is the most likely cause.
 The sophistication of the attacks led US officials to suspect a third country may have been involved with the incidents, but they have not been able to reach that conclusion.
   In  August 2017, officials included Russia among a list of countries that have an adversarial relationship with the United States and that American investigators suspected might be involved.
 Russia is one of the few countries that have used microwave technology before, and a theory among investigators was that some rogue Cuban intelligence officials worked with Russia because they were unhappy with the détente between United States and Cuba, a senior administration official said at the time.
title: 政府研究称，对美国外交官的声波袭击可能是微波能
title_en: Sonic attacks on US diplomats likely microwave energy, government study says

note:
欢迎来到“大声思考”，这是有关技术和哲学的新闻通讯。如果您想了解更多有关此子堆栈的信息，可以在这里进行。您也可以在下面订阅：
     在开始看起来像是一年一度的传统的过程中，我再次开始研究去中心化货币。这次，我专注于比特币。
 虽然我已经从技术上了解了比特币的基本知识（几年前甚至有人把它写成了我的博客文章），但由于以下原因，我几年前就将它作为一种货币注销了：
 它无法扩展（无论是在原始交易方面，还是由于为其供电所需的能源成本）
 炒作。我不喜欢匆忙时忙于事情。
 我喜欢的财经博客作者Lyn Alden最近在推特上发表了正面推文，加上Twitter的首席执行官Jack Dorsey最近一直在先行，所以我决定戳一下这只熊。
  这催生了许多疯狂的比特币狂热者，向我介绍了这一点，并为我提供了很多可供阅读的资源。自从我上次特别阅读比特币以来，发生了很多变化，所以，地狱，也许我的观点应该改变？ 
@evankozliner @maxkeiser这是我的文章：lynalden.com/invest-in-bitc…比特币可作为具有大量交易的基础/结算资产，可以在其上构建更高频率的层。这就是考虑其扩展性的方法。从宏观经济和针对特定协议的角度，以通俗的英语介绍如何以及为何投资比特币。 lynalden.com
 注意：我假设以下有关比特币的基本知识。我相信，如果有人尚未向您解释比特币，您已经可以找到一篇向您解释比特币的文章。 Lyn的文章对初学者来说也是不错的资源。
 我认为Lyn在关于为什么比特币的价格在未来几年内可能会上涨的文章非常引人注目。她说服了我，对于一个投资者-我认为一个拥有大量可支配收入的投资者-比特币可能是一个可靠的不对称押注，值得将其投资组合的1％左右投入。如果一些黄金爱好者只向其中注入一点现金，它就会虚度。而且事实证明她是正确的，最近比特币达到了新高。此外，考虑到大多数人期望在未来几年内看到的美元供应增加，鉴于其固定供应，比特币似乎是相当大的对冲。也许最重要的是，比特币经受了时间的考验。在几年前的大热潮中，大多数人（包括我自己）都会被另一种加密货币篡夺。事实并非如此，因此，它保持了其安全性的领先地位（对于比特币，它与采用紧密相关）。 Lyn描绘了一种可能的图景，说明在中期（几年之内）中，Bitcoin如何成为一项可靠的投资。
  但是，无论比特币是一种好的货币（可以长期用作真正的储备货币），还是由于通货膨胀回避而使比特币仅具有中期投资潜力，或者“大傻瓜理论”是两回事。 Lyn谈论投资，因此至少在我阅读的文章中，她避免了长期的猜测。
 很难低估货币与国家分离的重要性。而且，更难知道政府将如何应对。如果政府无法控制的固定资产比特币成为全球标准，那么法令的作用是什么？
 著名的对冲基金经理和作家雷·达里奥（Ray Dalio）似乎认为政府不会让这种情况发生：
  现代货币理论称，有很多原因使政府希望其货币具有灵活性。 MMT的悬崖峭壁版本是它允许拥有储备货币的政府（例如美国）控制价格稳定和就业，而不管它们承担的债务如何。以储备货币借款的其他国家则无法享受此项优惠；这只是MMT的众多批评之一。 
有一个共同的论点是，去中心化货币给通过MMT操纵法定货币的中央银行带来了问题。我的理解是，“正确”的大规模采用将需求从当前的世界储备转移到比特币，从而有效地颠覆了当今全球金融的运作方式，从而降低了国家对货币的权力。如今，尤其是在冠状病毒时代，“钱包的力量”似乎是无限的：美联储已向国会发出信号，表示愿意凭空赚钱购买几乎无限量的债务作为刺激。在比特币被大量采用的世界中，这种刺激措施根本不起作用，因为比特币的供应是固定的，而相比之下，法定货币（如美元）可能看起来像垄断货币。
 要获得MMT的出色概述，请阅读以下文章：现代货币理论（MMT）：概述。
 如今，对经济学最不感兴趣的人都知道，美元是世界储备货币。鉴于我们每月注入数万亿美元，许多人担心美元的稳定性。这就像去伊拉克，但每个月都去。伊拉克“仅为” 1.1万亿美元。像比特币这样的标准储备货币确实会给人们带来“摆脱困境”的作用，这是由于国家决策而导致的恶性通货膨胀和其他货币操纵问题。
  我仍在尝试弄清各国政府在比特币下到底会失去多少控制权，无论我能看到这是正面的还是负面的，以及我是否同意达里奥的观点，即各国政府将（或可能）采取行动来阻止这种情况。
 我仍在努力思考，大规模采用比特币可能对贷款或其他金融义务（例如和解）意味着什么。在我看来，债务的执行和追踪资金的能力就像其最重要的特性一样。如果我不知道某人有多少比特币，如何在法庭上使他们承担财务责任？也许今天我们已经在某种程度上遇到了这个问题，但是在我看来，比特币将大大扩大其范围。
 我也对固定汇率的世界的真实状况很感兴趣。我想通过法令来“夸大”债务人的义务并没有真正摆在桌面上。这种途径似乎可能会产生一些有趣的后果。
 中国公司拥有的哈希率（即比特币的开采）所占的百分比是另一个迫在眉睫的担忧，我对此并没有真正的答案（65％）。比特币的51％的哈希率是攻击者或国家行为者操纵网络所需要的。 
人们给我的关于比特币能源消耗的答案（这已经与瑞士相当）也不是很好。向我推荐的对此的一个回应归结为对抗与通胀“有价值”的气候变化所带来的好处。 “闪电”网络将交易作为中介进行结算的前景似乎是有希望的，但是我需要更多的研究。
 “为稳定的货币制度提供的经济稳定和经济自由付出代价；这是比特币应该和将要消耗的能源数量的真正理由。其他所有事情都会使人分心。”
  然而，尽管有上述担忧，我仍然可以想象一个场景，其中比特币或类似的数字货币将成为储备金。我有些人担心这是一个囚徒困境，在“比特币隧道”的尽头，我们最终都变得更糟。
 完成研究后，我应该在博客上发表最后一篇文章。我的直觉是，我离全面了解比特币还有很长的路要走。
    我们欠中国多少钱？ -《哈佛商业评论》对中国的外国贷款这一日益重要且不透明的问题进行了一些研究。
 现代货币理论：概述-可能是我所见过的MMT的最佳概述。
  美元是否已失去作为主要国际货币的地位？ -深入研究仅在COVID之前的IMF有关外汇储备的数据。对于那些开始涉足全球储备货币的运作方式以及俄罗斯和中国等不同参与者正在采取行动的人来说，这是一个有趣的读物。 
我投资比特币的3个原因-我在下面的第2部分中提到的Lyn Alden文章。精彩阅读。
  渐渐地，然后突然文章。我阅读了其中的一半内容，以期获得“比特币的观点”。这些是很受打击或错过的事情。在写有关比特币的“真实”博客文章之前，我可能仍会阅读其余内容。
  道德风景-萨姆·哈里斯（Sam Harris）概述了他的道德现实主义风格。我已经非常了解Sam在这个问题上的看法，但是，鉴于所有的比特币阅读情况，我想深入了解一下我最同意的内容。我正在考虑在这里写一篇文章来捍卫Sam的观点，因为我在网上看到几篇批评它的文章。
 语言，真相和逻辑-几年前，纳西姆·塔勒布（Nassim Taleb）将我转变为某种形式的经验主义，从那时起，我一直想通过它的一些较早版本进行工作，例如《逻辑实证主义》（Logical Positivism），这是本书的主题（撰写于1936年）。即使我不确定我是否完全同意，对数学专有真理的批评也特别有趣。但是，有时我迷失了作者的语言观点。奇怪的是，这本书是翻页机，而且启动时间很短！
 “无论我们想采取哪种情况，我们都将始终发现，逻辑或数学原理可能被混淆的情况是通过使原理不受质疑的方式解决的。”
  Spark：权威指南-最近，我开始在一家新公司担任数据工程师的工作，因此我一直在寻找Spark上的一些资源。本书的前几章内容写得很清楚；它绝对超越了我在网上可以找到的Spark上的其他资源。
 如何在加密的S3存储桶之间进行复制-我为《迈向数据科学》撰写的一篇文章，是我为工作而撰写的AWS IAM简介的后续文章。 IAM简介到达了r / aws的顶部。云安全性非常棘手。在AWS任职期间，我有一些非常出色的导师向我讲授了大多数基础知识，因此在在线入门级上谈论它时，我感到很自在。
note_en:
Welcome to Think Out Loud, a newsletter about technology and philosophy. If you’d like to learn more about this substack, you can do so  here. You can also subscribe below:
     In what has begun to look like an annual tradition, I’ve started researching decentralized currencies again. This time, I’m focused on Bitcoin.
 While I already understand the basics Bitcoin technically (some have even made it into  blog posts of mine a few years back) I’d written it off some years back as a currency due to:
 Its inability to scale out (both in terms of raw transactions, and due to the energy cost required to power it)
 The hype. I don’t like getting in on things when I feel rushed.
 A financial blogger I like, Lyn Alden, tweeted positively about it recently however, plus the CEO of Twitter, Jack Dorsey, keeps shilling it lately, so I decided to poke the bear.
  This spawned a lot of mad Bitcoin enthusiasts getting on me about this or that, and got me a host of resources to read up on. A lot has changed since I last read about Bitcoin in particular, so hell, maybe my opinion should change?
  @evankozliner  @maxkeiser Here's my article:  lynalden.com/invest-in-bitc…Bitcoin scales as a base/settlement asset with large transactions, upon which higher-frequency layers can be built. That's the way to think about its scaling.    A primer on how and why to invest in Bitcoin, from a macroeconomic and protocol-specific point of view, in plain English. lynalden.com
 Note: I assume some basic knowledge of Bitcoin below. I trust you can find an article explaining Bitcoin to you already, if someone hasn’t already explained it to you already. Lyn’s article is also a great resource for beginners.
 I think Lyn has a really compelling article as to why Bitcoin’s price could increase in the coming years. She convinced me that, for an investor—I would assume one with a lot of disposable income—Bitcoin may be a solid asymmetric bet, worth putting 1% or so of one’s portfolio into. If some of the gold enthusiasts pour just a little bit of cash into it, it could moon. And she’s being proven right, with Bitcoin reaching new highs recently. Further, considering the increase in dollar supply most folks are expecting to see in the next few years, Bitcoin seems like a considerable hedge, given its fixed supply. Perhaps most importantly, Bitcoin stood the test of time. During the big craze a few years back, most—including myself—figured Bitcoin would be usurped by another cryptocurrency. It was not, and hence, it retained its lead in security (which, for Bitcoin, is tied to adoption). Lyn paints a conceivable picture about how, in the medium term—a couple of years time—Bitcoin could be a solid investment.
  But whether Bitcoin is a good currency—one that could function as a true reserve currency long term—or if Bitcoin merely has medium-term investing potential due inflation dodging or the “Theory of the Larger Fool” are two separate things. Lyn talks about investing, so she shies away from long-term speculation, at least in the post I read.
 It’s tough to understate how pivotal the  separation of currency and state would be. And it’s tougher to know how governments would respond. What would the role of fiat be if Bitcoin, a fixed asset that governments could not control, were to become the global standard?
 Ray Dalio, a famous hedge fund manager and author, seems to think governments wouldn’t let this happen:
  There are a lot of reasons governments want flexibility over their currencies, a la Modern Monetary Theory. The cliff notes version of MMT is that it allows governments with reserve currencies, like the US, to control price stability and employment, irrespective of the debt they take on. Other countries that borrow in reserve currencies do not enjoy this benefit; this is just one of the many critiques of MMT.
 There’s a common line of reasoning arguing that decentralized currencies pose problems for central banks that manipulate fiat currency via MMT. My understanding is that “True” mass adoption would effectively upend the way global finance is done today by shifting demand from current world reserves to Bitcoin, thereby decreasing the power states have over currency. Today, especially in Coronavirus times, the “Power of the Purse” seems unlimited: the fed has signaled to congress that is willing to buy a  practically unlimited amount of debt as stimulus by generating dollars out of thin air. In a world under mass Bitcoin adoption, this kind of stimulus simply doesn’t work, as the supply of Bitcoin is fixed, and fiat currencies, like dollars, might look like monopoly money by comparison.
 For an excellent overview of MMT, read this piece:  Modern Monetary Theory (MMT): A General Introduction.
 Today, as most even mildly interested in economics know, the dollar is the world reserve currency. Many people are worried about the stability of the dollar, given that we’re injecting trillions of dollars monthly. This is like going to Iraq, but every month. Iraq was “only” 1.1 trillion dollars. A standard reserve currency, like Bitcoin, does give people an “escape hatch” from hyperinflation and other currency manipulation problems, stemming from state decision making.
  I’m still trying to piece together what exactly how much control governments really lose under Bitcoin, whether I can see that as a positive or a negative, and whether I agree with Dalio that governments would ( or   could) take action to prevent it.
 I’m still trying to wrap my head around what mass Bitcoin adoption might mean for lending, or other financial obligations, like settlements. The enforcement of debt and the ability to  track money seem, to me, like some of its most important properties. If I can’t tell how much Bitcoin someone has, how can they be made financially responsible in court? Perhaps we already have this problem today, to some extent, but it seems to me that Bitcoin would increase its scope greatly.
 I’m also interested in what a world with a fixed amount of currency really looks like. I would imagine “inflating away” debtors obligations through fiat would not really be on the table. This avenue seems like it might have some interesting consequences.
 The percentage of hash rate—the mining of Bitcoin—owned by Chinese firms is another looming concern I haven’t really seen an answer to either (65%). 51% of Bitcoin’s hash rate is what it would take it manipulate the network by an attacker or state actor.
 The answers folks have given me on Bitcoin’s energy consumption—which is already matching Switzerland’s—have not been good either. One response to this  recommended to me boiled down to the benefits of fighting inflation “worth” climate change. The promise of a “lightning” network that settles transactions as an intermediary seems promising, but requires more research on my end.
 “Put a price on economic stability and the economic freedom a stable monetary system provides; that is the true justification for the amount of energy bitcoin should and will consume. Everything else is a distraction.”
  And yet, despite the above concerns, I can still imagine a scenario where Bitcoin—or a digital currency like it—becomes a reserve. There’s a part of me that’s worried this is a prisoners dilemma, and, at the end of the “Bitcoin tunnel”, we all end up being worse off.
 I should have a final blog post once I’m finished researching. My hunch is I’m still a ways away from having a complete picture of Bitcoin.
    How much do we owe china? - Harvard Business Review has done some research into the increasingly important—and opaque—question of China’s foreign lending.
 Modern Monetary Theory: A General Introduction - Probably the best overview of MMT I’ve seen.
  Has the dollar lost ground as the dominant international currency? - A deep dive into the  IMF data regarding foreign exchange reserves just pre-COVID. An interesting read for those beginning to dip their toes into how global reserve currencies operate and the moves different players, like Russia and China, are making.
  3 Reasons I’m Investing in Bitcoin - The Lyn Alden article I mention in section 2, below. Wonderful read.
  Gradually, then suddenly articles. I read about half of these to try and get a “Bitcoiners perspective”. These are  very hit or miss. I’ll still probably read the rest before I write a “real” blog post on Bitcoin.
  The Moral Landscape - Sam Harris gives an overview of his flavor of moral realism. I was already very aware of Sam’s take on this question, but, given all the Bitcoin reading, I wanted to dive into something I knew I’d mostly agree with. I’m considering writing a post in defense of Sam’s argument here, as I’ve seen several posts online critical of it.
 Language, Truth, & Logic - Some years back Nassim Taleb converted me to some form of empiricism, and, since then, I’ve wanted to work my way through some of its older iterations, like Logical Positivism, the topic this book is on (written 1936). The critique of mathematical apropri truths was particularly interesting, even if I’m not sure I entirely agree. However, sometimes I got lost on the linguistic points the author made. This book is, weirdly, a page-turner, and very short to boot!
 “Whatever instance we care to take, we shall always find that the situations in which a logical or mathematical principal might appear to be confuted are accounted for in such a way as to leave the principal unassailed.”
  Spark: The Definitive Guide - I’ve started work as a data engineer at a new company recently, so I’ve been poking at some resources on Spark. This book’s first couple chapters are written very clearly; it’s definitely above and beyond the other resources on Spark I’ve been able to find online.
 How to copy between Encrypted S3 Bucket - An article I wrote for Towards Data Science as a follow up for an  AWS IAM introduction I wrote for work. The IAM introduction got to the  top of r/aws. Cloud security is pretty tricky. In my time at AWS, I had some pretty solid mentors that taught me most of the basics, so I felt comfortable talking about it at an introductory level online.
title: 比特币（仍然）没有意义

note:
作为一个数字营销商，Emily Ware在网上花费了大量时间，但这会带来风险。洁具患有边缘性人格障碍，这是一种与冲动行为有关的心理健康状况。就她而言，那是在网上花钱。
 她说：“到2020年初，我背负了4,250英镑的债务，没有什么可证明的。” “其中95％的原因是冲动性支出，从衣服到酒吧旅行到演出门票。最糟糕的情况之一是花300英镑购买门票，让雪儿心血来潮。”
  根据慈善机构“金钱与心理健康”（Money and Mental Health）的调查，八分之一的成年人在网上消费超出了他们的承受能力，而四分之一的成年人购买了他们不需要的东西。经历过精神健康问题的人发生这两种疾病的可能性是其两倍，该慈善机构表示，他们在冠状病毒大流行期间特别脆弱。
 去年118 118 Money的一项调查显示，英国人每月在冲动购买上的总支出高达10亿英镑。尽管心理健康，情绪和个性都在其中发挥作用，但越来越多的消费者心理被用来决定我们在网上的选择。设计与行为科学融为一体，这意味着我们计划外的购买并不总是像我们想象的那样自发。
  凯特·南丁格尔（Kate Nightingale）是一名消费者心理学家，与包括克拉娜（Klarna），施华洛世奇（Swarovski）和英国心脏基金会（British Heart Foundation）在内的品牌和慈善机构合作。她说，她的职责确实涉及做可以使人们购买的事情，“但我们要确保他们真正了解产品的含义，产品的价值”。
 无论它们是否用于积极手段，对于某些消费者而言，问题在于这些策略都是狡猾，成功且设计得不引人注目。很难说出品牌何时使用它们，并且很难抗拒最具吸引力的品牌。
  零售商利用心理学的最常见方式包括：催促我们做出决定，并说服我们如果不购物就错过了。 
稀缺性：任何有限的事情都会产生紧迫感，使我们能够迅速采取行动。根据定义销售是有限的。当酒店网站说有四个房间可用，但五个人正在查看它们时，也会使用此功能。之所以有效，是因为我们大多数人都希望避免失去某些东西的痛苦。
 免费送货的门槛以相同的方式运作。即使我们不需要或不需要额外的物品，错过免费赠品的痛苦也会使我们花费更多。
 社会证明：评论，推荐和喜欢表示产品已经过尝试和测试，因此感觉安全。之所以起作用，是因为我们是一群动物：我们互相复制以表明自己适合，并且因为复制是一个较容易的认知过程。
 一些品牌通过透露其他消费者购买的商品来利用这一点，而Facebook则突出显示您的朋友和家人喜欢的东西。为什么？因为我们最信任他们的意见，因此使其成为一种有效的营销方式。
 默认选项：坚持默认设置所需的精力较少，例如将新闻通讯框打勾。重复订阅（通常会打折以进一步提高交易价格）和精选套餐也是如此。另一方面，夜莺说，以后取消会比较困难，因为这意味着要面对您是否第一次选择不好。
 在家尝试选项按相同的按钮。这些使您可以订购多个项目，并返回不需要的项目。零售商知道，许多消费者将避免选择或寄回商品的痛苦。 Ware同意：“问题是我会保留超出预算的衣服。”
 取景：滚动浏览许多类似产品会很累，因此网站找到了阻止我们进行分区或单击的方法。 
夜莺说：“我们的大脑被设计为在某些不同的地方要注意，因为这可能是潜在的危险或机会。”框架化或划分内容也使理解变得容易，而不是将所有内容融合在一起。
 摩擦会阻止我们完成任务，可能意味着零售商损失收入
 她说，将字体大小仅改变一两点是另一种策略。 “下意识地，你的思想就会振作起来。而且由于它更大一些，您自然会注意它。因此，您会觉得自己喜欢这种产品，但与该产品无关。”
 无摩擦付款：一旦我们准备好花钱，无摩擦付款选项（如一键式付款和客人结帐）将帮助我们在第二次思考之前就结账。现在购买，以后再付款（如Klarna），也会给人一种付款灵活性的错觉因此，人们承受得起，同时淡化这是一种债务。评论家担心的是这种速度和缺乏透明度。
  理财和心理健康说，这些营销技巧都诱使消费者花费超出其承受能力的支出，并使他们容易遭受债务麻烦。该慈善机构呼吁零售商增加摩擦，以降低购物速度，提高购物速度。
 摩擦会使我们无法完成任务，可能意味着零售商损失收入。 Nightingale说：“以实际商店中的队列为例。” “因为排长队，您放弃了几次潜在的购买？”
 在线摩擦包括必须单击多个按钮或阅读小字体，而设计师通常会尽量减少这些事情。 Nightingale说，处理信息在精力和精力上都是昂贵的。 “我们的大脑通常正在寻找更简便的方法，因此可以为其他更重要的事情节省能量。” 
Sam Nurse是债务顾问和Money Advice Hub的主席。她说，冲动性购买与诸如租金之类的优先成本冲突时会引起问题。她建议那些在支出上挣扎的客户“努力使自己意识到任何冲动的购物习惯，并找到方法来消除这种冲动”。
 护士希望零售商采取“道德营销和广告政策，旨在不利用低收入群体和弱势消费者的冲动购买”。她和Ware都呼吁在线商店帮助消费者在付款之前评估可承受性和后果-但这与世界各地零售商在网上建立的感觉良好，省力的相反。
 Ware说：“大约六年前我刚开始从事营销工作时，我想现在我知道我无法接受的所有策略。” “我会看赌博网站的广告，并考虑他们的策略多么荒谬，而且我永远也不会被吸引，但是我会被一些令人上瘾的东西吸引。”
note_en:
A s a digital marketer, Emily Ware spends a lot of time online, yet this comes with a risk. Ware has  borderline personality disorder, a mental health condition linked with impulsive behaviours. In her case, that’s spending money online.
 “At the start of 2020 I was £4,250 in debt with nothing to show for it,” she says. “A good 95% of this was due to impulse spending, from clothes to pub trips to gig tickets. One of the worst was spending £300 on tickets to see Cher on a whim.”
  According to the charity  Money and Mental Health, one in eight adults spend more than they can afford to online, while almost one in four buy things they don’t need. People who have experienced mental health problems are twice as likely to do either of these, and the charity says they are  especially vulnerable during the coronavirus pandemic.
 Britons collectively spend up to £1bn every month on impulse buys,  according to a survey last year by 118 118 Money. While mental health, mood and personality all play a role in that, consumer psychology is increasingly being used to shape our choices online. This blend of design and behavioural science means our unplanned purchases aren’t always as spontaneous as we think.
  Kate Nightingale is a consumer psychologist and works with brands and charities including Klarna, Swarovski and the British Heart Foundation. She says her role does involve doing things that can make people buy, “but we’re making sure that they truly understand the meaning of the product, the value of it”.
 Whether they are used for positive means or not, the problem for some consumers is that these tactics are slick, successful and designed to be unobtrusive. It can be hard to tell when brands are using them, and tough to resist the most appealing.
  Some of the most common ways retailers tap into psychology include rushing us into decisions and persuading us that we are missing out if we don’t make a purchase.
 Scarcity: Anything limited creates a sense of urgency and primes us to act quickly. Sales are by definition limited. Hotel websites also use this when they say four rooms are available but five people are viewing them. It works because most of us want to avoid the pain of losing something.
 Thresholds on free shipping work the same way. The pain of missing out on a freebie can nudge us into spending a bit more, even if we do not need or want the extra items.
 Social proof: Reviews, recommendations and likes signal that a product is already tried and tested, so it feels safe. It works because we are a herd species: we copy each other to show we fit in, and because copying is an easier cognitive process.
 Some brands tap into this by revealing what other consumers bought, while Facebook highlights what your friends and family like. Why? Because we trust their opinions most of all, making it a potent form of marketing.
 Default choices: It takes less mental effort to stick with defaults, such as leaving the newsletter box ticked. The same applies to repeat subscriptions (often discounted to further sweeten the deal) and curated packages. On the flipside, Nightingale says cancelling later can feel harder as it means confronting whether you chose poorly the first time.
 Try-at-home options push the same buttons. These let you order multiple items and return whatever you don’t want. Retailers know many consumers will avoid the pain of choosing or sending things back. Ware agrees: “The problem was I would keep more clothes than I had the budget for.”
 Framing: Scrolling through lots of similar products gets tiring, so websites find ways to stop us zoning out or clicking away.
 “Our brain is designed to pay attention when something’s different because it can be a potential danger or opportunity,” Nightingale says. Framing or dividing content also makes it easier to make sense of, rather than everything blending in together.
 Friction is anything that stops us completing tasks and it can mean lost revenue for retailers
 Varying font sizes by only one or two points is another tactic, she says. “Subconsciously your mind will pick it up. And because it’s a little bit bigger, you will naturally pay attention to it. Therefore you feel that you like this product – but it has nothing to do with the product.”
 Frictionless paying: Once we are primed to spend, frictionless payment options such as one-click and guest checkout help us to check out before second thoughts kick in. Buy now, pay later  options, such as Klarna, also give the illusion of payment flexibility and, therefore, affordability, while playing down that it is a form of debt. It is this speed and lack of transparency that concerns critics.
  Money and Mental Health says these marketing tricks all tempt consumers to spend more than they can afford and leave them vulnerable to debt troubles. The charity is calling on retailers to make online shopping slower and more considered by adding friction back into the process.
 Friction is anything that stops us completing tasks and it can mean lost revenue for retailers. “Take an example of a queue in an actual store,” Nightingale says. “How many times did you drop your potential purchase because you were standing in a long queue?”
 Online friction includes having to click multiple buttons or read small print, things designers usually try to minimise. Processing information is expensive in terms of energy and effort, Nightingale says. “Our brain is generally looking for easier ways, so it can conserve energy for other things that are more important.”
 Sam Nurse is a debt adviser and the chair of  Money Advice Hub. She says impulse buys cause problems when they conflict with priority costs, such as rent. She advises clients struggling with their spending to “try to become self-aware of any impulse buying habits and find ways to disrupt the urge”.
 Nurse wants retailers to adopt “ethical marketing and advertising policies that aim not to exploit impulse buying among lower income groups and vulnerable consumers”. She and Ware are both calling on online stores to help consumers to assess affordability and consequences before paying – but that is the opposite of the feelgood, low-effort world retailers are building online.
 “When I first started in marketing around six years ago I thought now I know about all the tactics I wouldn’t be able to fall for them,” Ware says. “I’d look at ads for gambling sites and think how ridiculous their tactics are and that I would never be drawn in – but managed to be drawn in by something just as addictive.”
title: 零售商用来使我们增加支出的策略–以及它们如何伤害弱势群体
title_en: Tactics retailers use to make us spend more – and how they harm the vulnerable

note:
在COVID-19出现之前很久，收入（收入）和财富不平等问题就一直出现在我（可能是您）的雷达屏幕上。大流行使它们更加明显和紧急。美联储的行动扩大了差距。我们现在处在这样的情况下，社会的上层阶级可以轻松地保持安全和繁荣，而下层阶级则处于危险和危险的生活中。
  实际上比这更糟。高端是安全的，因为数以百万计的“基本”工人正在生产和交付我们需要的货物，使他们在过程中处于危险之中。在某种程度上，情况一直如此。现在更清楚了，赌注也更高了。
 两周前，我谈到了彼得·特尔钦（Peter Turchin）的“精英生产过剩”思想，该思想导致了社会和经济危机。我读的书听起来很不合时宜。进一步阅读不再令人感到安慰。尽管他没有任何解决方案，但Turchin有助于阐明我们如何达到这一点。今天，我们将更深入地思考其含义。如您所见，有很多。
  我们在社交媒体（甚至是匿名媒体）上看到的“模因”经常在泄露。这是今年的一轮比赛。
  据我所知，没有人能够验证这个故事或将其追溯到消息来源。可能并非如此，但这完全是合理的。我知道经历过这种经历的人。您也可能这样做。他们遵循了规则，把一切都做好了，然后癌症或其他一些危险因素摧毁了一切。一直发生。
 但这是事实：并非所有人都遇到这种情况。顶层，寓言中的1％或10％或您想划界的地方，当有人患癌症时，不要失去一切。用Peggy Noonan的术语来说，它们是“受保护的”类。该链接指向我在2016年写的一封信，其中描述了两个政党对他们所谓的“精英阶层”的挫败感。我谈到了受保护的阶层如何无法真正理解不受保护的阶层的胃reality现实，对于他们而言，仅一个轮胎，一次急诊室旅行或其他人身灾难就完全耗尽了他们的微薄积蓄。他们看着那些看起来能够负担得起他们所需要的东西的人，“受保护的阶级”，想知道为什么不是我吗？他们也想参加那个班级。
 但是加入，这并不容易，因为我已经感叹多年了。彼得·图尔钦（Peter Turchin）的工作使我更加担忧。他指出，纵观历史上的许多文明，繁荣会创造更多的财富和富裕的人民。问题在于他们的人数往往比“精英”职位或职位的人数增长更快。例如，在美国，我们有100名参议员和435名众议院议员。这些数字是固定的，而认为自己有资格担任职务的人数则在增加。因此，我们庞大的chat不休阶级在没有任何真正力量的情况下绕着华盛顿绕道而行。华尔街也有类似的模式。大型公司，大学，州和地方政府（基本上是构成社会骨干的所有机构）也是如此。 
同时，下面的一两层是想加入高层的人数众多。他们认为教育是关键。获得大学学位，成功之门将打开。也没错。但是在这里，并非所有学位都是平等的。在很大程度上取决于您在哪里上大学以及在那儿见谁。成绩不是唯一的因素。
 无论如何，数以百万计的人试图获得这些学位都背负着沉重的债务。许多人发现自己没有理想的文凭，但仍然背负着债务。不只是大学学位人们陷入债务寻求法律，MBA，医学和其他他们认为会为他们打开希望之门的证书。宽松的货币政策在鼓励和鼓励这些雄心壮志的同时，又抬高了资产价格，这使它们变得难以实现，即使不是不可能。
 因此，您最终得到了一群潜在的受过良好教育的精英，他们无法达到或维持他们认为应有的地位，而另一半的精英阶层则要求同样的地位。 Turchin说，下一步是让一些心灰意冷的精英成为“反精英”，并与下层阶级结盟，然后寻求上台。想想2016年，当时英国举行了英国脱欧公投，而美国则看到特朗普赢得了白宫。世界各地的民粹主义运动在某种程度上是精英冲突的体现。
  我的一些批评家（顺便说一句，我深爱着那些人）指责我认为央行是万恶之源。我根本不这样认为。我相信我们需要中央银行，而不是我们目前正在获得的政策。我认为，央行行动不当会导致和/或加剧许多问题。
 现在困扰我们的收入和财富不平等在很大程度上是持久和人为地低利率的结果。这可以追溯到大金融危机之前。这是自1955年以来由美联储直接控制的联邦基金利率。
  您可以看到，自1980年代初以来，费率一直在下降。我认为，在同一时期，工资增长落后于生产率增长，并且最富有的美国人和中产阶级之间的差距拉大并非偶然。
 如果遵循该过程，这很容易理解。利率是金钱的价格，金钱可以帮助您赚到更多。因此，降低其价格是最有能力进行投资的人的礼物。这是一份投资通讯，因此您阅读的大多数人都可能属于该类别。但美国大部分地区并非如此。 
直到最近，大多数美国人所做的最复杂的“投资”还是拥有一个银行储蓄账户，或者也许是一张存款证明。长期以来，这就足够了。一个普通的人可以节省每笔薪水中的一点钱，无风险地获得5％或7％的收益，并在房子上积build积蓄或首付。现在这是不可能的。您很幸运能从银行获得0.1％的收益。
 这种“金融压制”具有各种负面影响。首先，它鼓励过度消费。当您没有任何奖励时，为什么还要保存任何东西？轻松度假或购买更好的汽车。那些保存任何东西的人都不得不冒险可能没有做好准备。我们知道通常如何结束。
 因此，随着时间的流逝，越来越多的人到了中年或退休年龄，几乎没有积蓄甚至没有积蓄（并且经常背负很多债务），并发现他们被告知无法实现的生活方式。这是引起不满和不满的秘诀。不足为奇，我们都有很多。
  同样低的利率也促使投机活动抬高了资产价格，尤其是住房。因此，低收入人群不得不花更多的钱去睡觉。这对于房东至少在一段时间内从他们那里收取租金是有好处的。同样适用于大学管理人员和贸易学校，它们有机会爬上梯子，而且要付出一定的代价。
 同样，低利率补贴了工作自动化。从长远来看，这实际上对工人有好处，但从现在到现在，这会减少工作并压低工资。因此，中产阶级发现自己减少了支出，增加了支出。那不可能无限期地持续下去。
 现在输入COVID-19。这种大流行像中子弹一样打击了我们的经济，消灭了数百万个低薪服务工作，同时使受保护阶层不受伤害，在某些情况下实际上更好。甚至营救也得不到平衡。
 失业的饭店工人获得了几个月的慷慨失业救济金，现在已经到期（并且可能不会延长）。 
小型企业主获得了PPP“可宽恕贷款”，其中包含复杂的规则和对支出方式的严格限制（许多不能迅速申请的贷款甚至没有得到）。
 得益于美联储（和国会），大型企业获得了数万亿美元的几乎免费现金，并且可以随意使用这些现金。在许多情况下，这意味着股票回购和高管奖金。
 人们想加入精英组织，对他们不能生气感到惊讶吗？ Turchin所描述的过程使我们的当前情况适合于T。而它们恰恰是在我们已经以许多其他方式痛苦地分裂时。
 卡梅伦·默里（Cameron Murray）在对Turchin最新书籍的评论中，提供了以下预告性段落：
 [T]这是我们看到的一种在整个历史中反复出现的模式，当一个成功的帝国扩展其疆界至今，使其成为街区上最大的孩子时。当生存不再受到威胁时，自私的精英和其他特殊利益集团将占领政治议程。 “我们都在同一条船上”的精神消失了，取而代之的是“赢家通吃”的心态。随着精英阶层的不断壮大，其他人口越来越贫穷。贫富悬殊的贫富差距进一步侵蚀了合作。
 在过去的几个世纪和千年中，缺乏内部合作邀请了好斗的邻居占领了这个杂乱无章的国家。罗马并没有因此而被“野蛮人”所征服，而是因为它自杀了，当把它凝聚在一起的胶粘剂，所有派别的合作完全消失了。
 加里·诺斯（Gary North，我最早的真正历史导师之一）在2011年的一篇冗长而技术性的文章中谈到了著名的历史学家罗伯特·尼斯贝特（Robert Nisbet）。 
1953年，他的[尼斯特贝（Nisbet）]书《寻求社区》由牛津大学出版社出版。它得到了一些关注，大部分都受到好评，但它几乎不是畅销书。他问了以下问题：＆＃34;为什么现代世界在20世纪中叶转向极权主义？在产生极权主义的社会中发生了什么？他得出结论，这与社会秩序的崩溃有关。在整个历史中，人们效忠的那些机构，例如家庭，教会，行会，兄弟会以及类似的志愿机构，在二十世纪的重要性已逐渐消失。这只留下了孤立的个人和现代民族国家。人们通过参与群众运动政治而获得归属感。极权主义领导人开始吸引那些被孤立的人，即使他们生活在大城市中。这些领导人为数百万在城市中感到孤独的人们提供了兄弟情谊。现代的极权国家代替了家族，教会和志愿组织，这些组织在几千年的历史中已经赋予人们一种目标感和参与感。因此，从制度上来讲，极权主义是从激进的个人主义诞生的，尽管作为一种哲学，极权主义是完全反对个人主义的。
 听起来有点熟？在身份政治不断发展的世界中，情况可能并不相同，但肯定是押韵。诺斯接着分析了另一位同样著名的历史哲学家雅克·巴尔赞（Jacques Barzun），他在2000年以93岁的作品创作了最后的作品（他的第一本书是1927年出版的）：
 他问了一个问题：＆＃34;什么造就了一个国家？他回答了自己的问题。该问题的很大一部分答案是：共同的历史记忆。当国家的历史在学校中讲得不好，被年轻人忽略，被有资格的长者自豪地拒绝时，对传统的意识就在于要破坏它。 Nisbet在20年前提出了同样的观点。
 因此，我们拆毁了我们的开国元勋，甚至是反奴隶运动英雄的纪念碑，因为我们没有人的进步感，只关心今天的不满
  我想以积极的语调结束这些信件。如果这一进程能够像Turchin所说的那样展开，我们将没有什么值得庆祝的。但这并不意味着我们都注定了。让墙壁塌陷在您的顶部不同于让墙壁塌陷在您的周围。后者是可以生存的，尽管很难。
 尼尔·豪（Neil Howe），乔治·弗里德曼（George Friedman），彼得·图尔钦（Peter Turchin）（也许我也谦虚地包括我自己），都指出2020年代是一个充满压力，动荡的时期。但是，我们也希望以后会有一段平静的时期，这种团结感与第二次世界大战后的几年一样。
 您一定不要让自己陷入政治党派主义和社会焦虑之中，以免您忽略了“这也将过去”这一事实。我们生活在一个充满变革的时代。如果您专注于吸引注意力的媒体，您将错过真正重要的事件，这些事件实际上将改变社会并改善我们的生活。您应该参与的事情就是要确保您和您的家人不仅生存，而且在新的黄金时代繁荣昌盛。 
不用说，没人会想回到2020年，但我相信在2030年代，没人会想回到任何以前的时期。显然，我们必须解决许多问题。我们可能会这样做，也许会使用格言（通常归因于温斯顿·丘吉尔），“美国人在尝试了所有其他方法之后总会做正确的事情。”
 因此，我写这封信充满了警告，而并非完全是“感觉良好的材料”。我们需要认识到我们在未来的地图上所处的位置。我们还必须认识到我们还没有到达。我们正在旅途中。正如威廉·F·巴克利（William F. Buckley）在发起《国家评论》时所说的那样，现在该紧握腰部并打好仗了：“坚决制止历史，大喊大叫！”
 数十年后的那条路线应该不仅仅是一个保守的话题。无论是在政治上是左派还是右派，它都应该在那些相信美国实验的正确性的人的口中。当我说我们正在做的事情在政治，经济，社会或文化上都没有用时，我认为我不会太过分。关于受保护与不受保护的担忧是真实的。变革步伐的显着加快令许多人感到不安，实际上迫使许多生活方式发生了变化。要营造一种“我们在一起的感觉”的感觉，不仅在美国，而且在全球，还有许多工作要做。
 我们正在进入一个十年，在这个十年中，许多墙将倒塌。目标是当时不属于他们。这将要求我们保持敏捷和知情。我目前正在重新调整我的所有业务和投资以度过这段时间，建议您也这样做。
 说到这一点，我们将在短期内简短地重新开放Mauldin经济学Alpha协会的会员资格，这是获得我们所有研究成果的最经济有效的方式，并且还使您可以独家获得一些额外的利益。尽快查找更多详细信息。
  ARK投资管理公司的创始人凯瑟琳·伍德（Catherine Wood）是我2020年虚拟战略投资会议的亮点。我被她迷住了。 ARK是一家全球投资公司，专门致力于人工智能，DNA测序，机器人技术，能量存储和区块链技术等领域的颠覆性创新。他们寻求投资于明天的领先技术，这些突破将改变并改善我们的生活。借助AI，DNA测序的基因编辑和免疫疗法，自适应机器人，神经网络和为汽车和房屋提供动力的自主电池，实现区块链技术中的无摩擦转移，ARK看到了移动互联设备的巨大变化。
 会议结束后，我和我的投资管理团队联系了Cathie，问她是否愿意与我们合作。具体来说，任何人都可以购买她的ETF，它们非常好。我们对她的最高信念股票想法感兴趣。在与她和她的团队进行了几个月的尽职调查之后，我们很高兴能够通过CMG Mauldin投资组合平台上的管理帐户提供对ARK最高信念的前10名股票的访问权限。我鼓励您单击此处，提供一些基本的联系信息，CMG Mauldin的团队将向您发送指向凯瑟琳会议演讲视频的链接，以及对该策略及其最高信念的描述。我认为您真的很喜欢观看采访并向凯蒂学习。 （全部披露：我是CMG的首席经济学家。MauldinEconomics，LLC与CMG没有任何关系。） 
在波多黎各，州长刚刚发布了一系列新的限制，以期减少COVID-19案件。 无论他们是否做到这一点，他们肯定会对经济和个人施加压力。 特别是小型企业，再一次是那些不受保护的企业。 出于健康和经济原因，我们需要尽快购买疫苗。  我在上面谈论的危机？ 我已经写了好几次关于我们将如何“思考不可思议的东西”的文章。 这将成为2020年代的口头禅。 我希望我们将做比我们任何人都想象的更多的事，这将使我们所有人感到非常不舒服。 但是我们还是必须这样做。  然后，我将点击发送按钮。 我相信您会度过愉快的一周并保持安全。
note_en:
Growing income and wealth inequality were on my (and probably your) radar screen long before COVID-19 came along. The pandemic has made them both more obvious and more urgent. The actions by the Federal Reserve have widened the gap. We are now in a situation where society’s upper echelon can easily stay safe and prosperous while the lower segments live precariously and dangerously.
  It’s actually worse than that. The upper end is safe precisely  because millions of “essential” workers are producing and delivering the goods we need, placing themselves at risk in the process. That’s always been the case to some degree. Now it is clearer, and the stakes are higher.
 Two weeks ago I talked about Peter Turchin’s idea of  “elite overproduction” leading to social and economic crisis. What I read sounded disturbingly like our present situation. Further reading wasn’t any more comforting. While he doesn’t have any solutions, Turchin helps illuminate how we reached this point. Today we’ll go a little deeper and think about the implications. As you’ll see, there are many.
  The “memes” we see on social media, even the anonymous ones, are often revealing. Here’s one that made the rounds this year.
  As far as I can tell, no one has been able to verify this story or trace it to the source. It might not be true but it’s completely plausible. I know people who went through such experiences. You probably do, too. They followed the rules, did everything right, then cancer or some other bolt from the blue demolished everything. Happens all the time.
 But here’s the thing: This doesn’t happen to everyone. The top layer, the fabled 1% or 10% or wherever you want to draw the line, don’t lose everything when somebody gets cancer. They are, to use Peggy Noonan’s term,  a “protected” class. The link is to a letter I wrote in 2016, describing the frustration in both political parties with what they considered “the elites.” I talked about how the protected class doesn’t really understand the stomach-churning reality of the unprotected class for whom just one blown tire, a trip to the emergency room, or other personal disaster completely wipes out their meager savings. They look at those who appear able to afford whatever they need, “the protected class,” and wonder why is that not me? They want to be in that class, too.
 But it’s not easy to join, as I’ve been lamenting for years. Peter Turchin’s work adds to my concern. He notes, looking at many civilizations through history, that prosperity creates more wealth and wealthy people. The problem is their numbers tend to grow faster than the number of “elite” positions or jobs. In the US, for instance, we have 100 senators and 435 House members. Those numbers are fixed while the number of people who think they are qualified to hold office grows. Hence our large chattering class that circles the Washington beltway without any real power. Wall Street has a similar pattern. So do large companies, universities, state and local governments—basically all the institutions that compose society’s backbone.
 Meanwhile, a layer or two below are large numbers of people who want to join the top ranks. They think education is the key. Get a college degree and the doors to success will open. That’s not wrong, either. But here again, not all degrees are equal. Much depends on where you go to college and who you meet there. Grades aren’t the only factor.
 Regardless, millions load up on debt trying to get those degrees. Many find themselves without the desired diplomas but still saddled with the debt. And it’s not just college degrees; people go into debt seeking law, MBA, medical, and other credentials they think will open the desired doors. Loose monetary policy subsidizes and encourages these ambitions while simultaneously inflating asset prices, which make them difficult, if not impossible to achieve.
 So you end up with a group of potential educated elites who can’t attain or maintain the status they think they deserve, and another group below them demanding that same status. Turchin says the next step is for some of the disaffected elites to become “counter-elites” and make alliances with the lower classes, which then seek to take power. Think of 2016, when the UK had the Brexit referendum and the US saw Trump win the White House. Populist movements around the world are partly a manifestation of elite conflict.
  Some of my critics (whom I love dearly, by the way) accuse me of thinking central banks are the root of all evil. That’s not what I think at all. I believe we need central banks, just not the policies we are currently getting. I believe ill-considered central bank actions cause and/or aggravate many problems.
 The income and wealth inequality that now plague us are, to a great degree, the result of persistently and artificially low interest rates. This goes back long before the Great Financial Crisis. Here’s the Federal Funds Rate, which the Fed directly controls, since 1955.
  You can see rates have been generally falling since the early 1980s. I think it is not coincidence this is the same period in which wage growth lagged productivity growth, and the gap widened between the wealthiest Americans and the middle class.
 This is easy to understand if you follow the process. Interest rates are the price of money, and money helps you make more money. Hence, lowering its price is a gift to those who are in the best position to invest it. This is an investment newsletter, so most of you reading it are probably in that category. But most of America isn’t.
 Until recently, the most sophisticated “investing” most Americans did was to have a bank savings account or maybe a certificate of deposit. And for a long time, that was enough. An average person could save a little of every paycheck, make risk-free 5% or 7% yields, and build up a nest egg or the down payment on a house. That is now impossible. You are lucky to get 0.1% from your bank.
 This “financial repression” has all kinds of negative effects. For one, it encourages excess consumption. Why save anything when you get no reward? Easier to go on vacation or buy a nicer car. Those who do save anything have to take risks for which they may not be prepared. We know how that usually ends.
 So over time, a growing number of people reach middle age or retirement age with little or no savings (and often a lot of debt) and find the kind of lifestyle they were told to expect isn’t possible. This is a recipe for discontent and resentment. No surprise, we have plenty of both.
  Those same low interest rates encourage speculation that raises asset prices, particularly for housing. So lower-income people have to spend more just for a place to sleep. This is good for the landlords collecting rent from them, at least for a while. Ditto for the university administrators and trade schools that offer a chance to climb the ladder—for a price.
 Similarly, low interest rates subsidize job automation. In the long run, that’s actually good for workers, but in the here-and-now it eliminates jobs and suppresses wages. So the middle class finds itself making less and spending more. That can’t go on indefinitely.
 Now bring in COVID-19. This pandemic hit our economy like a neutron bomb, wiping out millions of low-wage service jobs while leaving the protected classes unharmed and in some cases actually better. And even the rescue wasn’t even-handed.
 Jobless restaurant workers got a few months of generous unemployment benefits, which have now expired (and may not be extended).
 Small business owners got PPP “forgivable loans” that came with complex rules and strict limits on how they could spend it (and many who couldn’t apply quickly didn’t even get that).
 Large businesses got, thanks to the Fed (and Congress), trillions in practically free cash and permission to spend it however they wanted. In many cases, that meant share buybacks and executive bonuses.
 Is it any wonder people want to join the elite, and are angry they can’t? The process Turchin describes fits our present circumstances to a T. And they come at a time when we are already bitterly divided in so many other ways.
 Cameron Murray, in a  review of  Turchin’s latest book, offers this foreboding paragraph:
 [T]here is a pattern that we see recurring throughout history, when a successful empire expands its borders so far that it becomes the biggest kid on the block. When survival is no longer at stake, selfish elites and other special interest groups capture the political agenda. The spirit that “we are all in the same boat” disappears and is replaced by a “winner take all” mentality. As the elites enrich themselves, the rest of the population is increasingly impoverished. Rampant inequality of wealth further corrodes cooperation.
 In past centuries and millennia, the lack of internal cooperation invited aggressive neighbors to conquer the disorganized country. Rome was not so much conquered by “barbarians” as it committed societal suicide, when the glue that held it together, the cooperation of all factions, simply disappeared.
 Gary North, (one of my first true historical mentors), in a  long and technical 2011 essay, talked about the famous historian Robert Nisbet.
 In 1953, his [Nisbet’s] book, The Quest for Community , was published by Oxford University Press. It received some attention, mostly favorable, but it was hardly a bestseller. He asked these questions: "Why was it that the modern world had turned to totalitarianism in the middle of the 20th century? What had taken place in the societies that gave birth to totalitarianism?" He concluded that it had to do with the breakdown of social order. Those institutions to which men had given allegiance throughout history, such as the family, the church, the guild, the fraternal order, and similar voluntary institutions, had faded in importance in the twentieth century. This left only the isolated individual and the modern nation-state. Men gained a sense of belonging through their participation in mass-movement politics. Totalitarian leaders began to attract individuals who were isolated, even though they were living in large cities. These leaders were able to offer a sense of brotherhood to millions of people who felt alone in the midst of cities. The modern totalitarian state functioned as a substitute for the family, church, and voluntary associations that for millennia had given people a sense of purpose and participation. So, totalitarianism was born out of radical individualism, institutionally speaking, even though as a philosophy, totalitarianism is completely opposed to individualism.
 Sound familiar? In a world of increasing identity politics, it may not be the same, but it certainly rhymes. North went on to analyze another equally famous philosopher of history, Jacques Barzun, who wrote his final opus at 93 in 2000 (his first book was in 1927):
 He asks the question: "What makes a nation?" He answers his own question. "A large part of the answer to that question is: common historical memories. When the nation's history is poorly taught in schools, ignored by the young, and proudly rejected by qualified elders, awareness of tradition consists only in wanting to destroy it." Nisbet had made the same point two decades earlier.
 Thus we tear down the monuments to our founding fathers and even the heroes of the anti-slavery movements because we have no sense of human progress, only a concern for the grievances of today
  I like to end these letters on a positive note. We won’t have much to celebrate if the process unfolds the way Turchin says it historically does. But that doesn’t mean we are all doomed. Having the walls collapse on top of you isn’t the same as having them collapse  around you. The latter is survivable, though hardly pleasant.
 Neil Howe, George Friedman, Peter Turchin, (and may I humbly include myself), all point to the 2020s as a stressful, tumultuous period. But we also expect a period of calm afterward, a sense of unity not unlike the post-World War II years.
 You must not let yourself get so caught up in political partisanship and societal angst that it takes your eyes off the fact that “this too shall pass.” We live in an age of wonderful transformation. If you focus on attention-grabbing media you will miss the truly important events, the things that will actually change society and make our lives better. The very things you should be participating in to make sure that you and your family not only survive but prosper in a new golden age.
 While it goes without saying that nobody will want to go back to the year 2020, I believe in the 2030s no one will want to go back to any prior time period. Clearly, we have a number of things that have to be sorted out. We will do that, perhaps using the maxim (often attributed to Winston Churchill), “Americans always do the right thing after they’ve tried everything else.”
 And so I write this letter full of warnings and not exactly “feel-good material” We need to recognize where we are on the map to the future. We must also recognize we have not arrived. We are on a journey. It is time to gird up our loins and fight the good fight, to, as William F. Buckley said when launching the  National Review: “Stand athwart history and yell stop!”
 Decades later that line should be more than a conservative talking point. It needs to be on the lips of those who believe in the rightness of the American experiment, whether politically left or right. I don’t think I am going too far out on a limb when I say that what we’re doing is not working: politically, economically, socially, or culturally. The concerns over the protected versus the unprotected are real. The remarkably accelerating pace of change is unsettling to many, and is in fact forcing a change in many lifestyles. There is much work to be done, not just in the US but globally, to create a sense of “we’re all in this together.”
 We are entering a decade in which many walls will come down. The goal is to not be under them at the time. That will require us to stay nimble and informed. I’m presently recalibrating all my businesses and investments to get through this time, and I suggest you do the same.
 Speaking of which, we will shortly, and briefly, reopen membership to the Mauldin Economics Alpha Society, which is the most cost-effective way to get all our research and also gives you exclusive access to some extra benefits. Look for more details soon.
  Catherine Wood, founder of ARK Investment Management, was a highlight of my 2020 Virtual Strategic Investment Conference. I was spellbound listening to her. ARK is a global investment firm solely focused on disruptive innovation in things like artificial intelligence, DNA sequencing, robotics, energy storage, and blockchain technology. They seek to invest in tomorrow’s leading technologies—the breakthroughs that will change and improve our lives. ARK sees a dramatically different future with mobile connected devises thanks to AI, gene editing and immunotherapies from DNA sequencing, adaptive robots, neural networks and autonomous batteries that power cars and houses to frictionless transfers in blockchain technology.
 After the conference, I and my investment management team reached out to Cathie and asked her if she would be willing to work with us. Specifically, anyone can buy her ETFs and they are very good. We were interested in her highest-conviction stock ideas. After several months of due diligence work with her and her team, we are thrilled to be able to offer access to ARK’s highest-conviction top-10 stocks via a managed account on the CMG Mauldin Portfolios Platform. I encourage you to  click here, provide some basic contact information, and my team at CMG Mauldin will send you a link to the video of Catherine’s conference presentation along with a description of the strategy and her highest-conviction ideas. I think you’ll really enjoy watching the interview and learning from Cathie. (Full disclosure: I am the chief economist for CMG. Mauldin Economics, LLC is not affiliated in any way with CMG.)
  Here in Puerto Rico the governor just issued a series of new restrictions to hopefully reduce COVID-19 cases. Whether they accomplish that or not, they will certainly put stress on the economy and individuals. Especially small businesses and, again, those in the unprotected class. We need the vaccines ASAP, for both health and economic reasons.
 The crises I talked about above? I’ve written several times on how we will have to “Think the Unthinkable.” That is going to be the mantra for the 2020s. I expect we will be doing the unthinkable more than any of us can imagine and it will make us all very uncomfortable. But we must do it anyway.
 And with that I will hit the send button. I trust you will have a good week and stay safe.

东，＆＃34;人造太阳，＆＃34;是ITER的全球同行，并希望达到一些相同的里程碑。
   官方媒体今天报道，中国已经开启了创纪录的“人造太阳”托卡马克。这是中国希望与全球国际热核实验反应堆（ITER）计划所计划的时间表相似的时间表。
  HL-2M托卡马克自2006年以来已经过迭代，但是今天的启动代表了实验高级超导托卡马克（EAST）团队经过多年的计划和工作，迈向真正的聚变点火的道路。
  2018年，当托卡马克达到1.8亿度时，EAST成为新闻。 2019年，EAST将这艘船进一步推开，并宣布计划在2020年将温度翻一番，达到托卡马克的主要工作温度为3.6亿度。这还没有发生，但是还有时间在2020年，而COVID-19已经影响了今年全球的科学进步。如果EAST团队晚了几个月，我们仍然可以认为这是一场胜利。
 此内容是从{embed-name}导入的。您可能可以在其网站上找到其他格式的相同内容，或者可以找到更多信息。
  像世界上许多托卡马克实验一样，EAST之前已经达到融合。作为一种清新的东西，在托卡马克的甜甜圈形（有时是球形）中，太阳热的等离子体绕圈旋转，并由过冷的电磁体保持在适当的位置。
 该磁场是漂浮在3.6亿度等离子体与一群显然无法承受该温度的人造材料之间的唯一事物。等离子体是通过将不同的核粉碎在一起，融合而不是分裂而产生的。 
当EAST于2006年建成时，该小组的研究人员开始了一系列不断升级的实验。这部分是简单的概念证明，因为托卡马克内部的温度在地球上几乎是空前的，至少在人类世时期是这样。
 随着温度的升高，磁性容器也必须增加，这已成为这些反应堆发生故障的关键点（或至少是“挑战”）。将每个实验运行推高一点并更大一些，使研究人员能够继续支撑外部零件。
  这意味着这些托卡马克反应堆的外部腔室通常本身就是低温冷却的杰作，能够承受世界上几乎任何其他事物都无法屈服的条件。即使数十年来世界上最聪明的人都在致力于这个想法，科学家们仍然没有创造出可生产的等离子体。
 EAST在2018年达到了等离子10秒，这是一个重要的里程碑。但这只是非常非常的开始。 。 。一开始。
  既然EAST已经按照制造商的说法进行了交易，那么该项目还有很多值得证明的事情。使托卡马克反应堆的整个组件加速运转需要花费大量的能量。如果聚变反应堆无法轻易超过该输入，它将永远不会产生动力，更不用说聚变支持者数十年来所售出的几乎无限的动力了。
 ITER和EAST紧密合作，除自身的融合项目外，中国也是ITER突破性合作的一部分。在生产出第一兆瓦的电力之前，这种合作是否价值数百亿美元？
 我们很快就会找到答案-至少五年后会发现。好的，十个上衣。

note_en:
EAST, an " artificial sun," is global peers with  ITER and hopes to reach some of the same milestones.
   China has  switched on its record-setting “ artificial sun” tokamak, state media reported today. This begins a timeline China hopes will be similar to the one planned by the global International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor ( ITER) project.
  The HL-2M tokamak has been iterated since 2006, but today's switch-on represents the Experimental Advanced Superconducting Tokamak (EAST) team’s road to true fusion ignition after years of planning and work.
  In 2018, EAST made news when the tokamak reached 180 million degrees. In 2019, EAST pushed the boat out further and announced plans to double that temperature in 2020—reaching the tokamak's prime operating temperature of 360 million degrees. This hasn't happened yet, but there’s still time in 2020, and COVID-19 has affected all the world’s scientific progress this year. If the EAST team is a few months late, we’ll still count that as a win.
 This content is imported from {embed-name}. You may be able to find the same content in another format, or you may be able to find more information, at their web site.
  Like many of the world’s tokamak experiments, EAST has reached fusion before. As a refresher, inside the donut-shaped (or, sometimes, more spherical) containment of a tokamak, sun-hot plasma swirls in a circle that’s held in place by supercooled electromagnets.
 This magnetic field is the only thing floating between 360-million-degree plasma and a bunch of human-made materials that obviously can’t sustain that temperature. The plasma results from smashing different nuclei together, fusing them rather than splitting them.
     When EAST was built in 2006, the team’s researchers began an escalating series of experiments. Part of this is simple proof of concept, because the temperatures inside tokamaks are almost unprecedented on Earth, period—at least on the surface during the Anthropocene.
 As temperatures climb, the magnetic containment must also increase, and this has been a key point of failure (or at least “challenge”) for these reactors. Pushing each experimental run a little bit hotter and bigger has let researchers continue to shore up the external parts.
  This means the outside chambers of these tokamak reactors are usually cryogenically cooled masterpieces in their own right, able to withstand conditions that would buckle almost anything else in the world. And even with the best minds in the world working on this idea for decades, scientists still haven’t made  productive plasma.
 EAST reached plasma for 10 seconds in 2018, which is a major milestone. But it’s just the very, very beginning . . .  of the beginning.
  Now that EAST has switched on for what its makers say is the real deal, the project has a lot to prove. It costs a huge amount of energy input to bring a tokamak reactor’s entire assembly up to speed. If a fusion reactor can’t easily outpace that input, it will never produce power, let alone the dream of virtually limitless power that fusion proponents have sold for decades.
 ITER and EAST work closely together, and China is part of the groundbreaking ITER collaboration in addition to its own fusion projects. Is that cooperation worth tens of billions of dollars before the first megawatt of power is ever produced?
 We’ll find out very soon—or at least in five years. Okay, 10 tops.

title: 中国已启动其HL-2M EAST托卡马克聚变反应堆

导演理查德·罗德里格斯（Richard Rodriguez）的奇幻冒险孩子3D影片《鲨鱼少年》和《熔岩女孩》首映已经15年了。这部电影曾被誉为2005年的电影盛会。虽然没有达到预期，但罗德里格斯显然对这个虚构的世界保持着深厚的爱慕-深得足以让他渴望与即将上映的Netflix独立续集《我们可以成为英雄》重新审视。是的，最初的Lavagirl，泰勒·杜利（Taylor Dooley）出场了。
  罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）是一位令人印象深刻的多才多艺的导演，从他的早期西方/动作片《玛利亚奇》（1992年），《绝望之城》（1995年）和《从黄昏到黎明》（1996年），到《间谍儿童》系列和去年的科幻大片，艾丽塔：战斗天使。在2003年的《间谍儿童3D：游戏结束》取得成功之后，罗德里格斯推出了另一款身临其境的儿童游戏。电影，根据他的小儿子Racer Max的故事改编。它的特色是一个名叫马克斯（Max）的小男孩，他的父母忽视了这个男孩，并在学校被欺负，他在他的日记中创建了一个虚构的梦想世界，名为《行星流口水》。其中的居民包括海洋生物学家的儿子鲨鱼（Taylor Lautner），以及倾向于纵火的Lavagirl（Dooley），以及＆＃34; plughounds＆＃34;。唱着LaLas的泡泡。但是当学校的恶霸偷走了麦克斯的梦想日记时，那些梦想开始流血变为现实。
 这部电影大多受到负面评价-在烂番茄上的最新评级只有19％，这主要归功于糟糕的3D效果和故事情节（毕竟这是7岁孩子的创意）。但是《鲨鱼男孩和熔岩女孩历险记》在全球的票房收入达到了7200万美元（是其预算的两倍），视觉效果令人印象深刻，劳特纳将继续担任《暮光之城》系列的主角。 （由于时间安排上的冲突，他无法出现在《我们可以成为英雄》中。）罗德里格斯还根据人物角色合编了一系列儿童小说。现在他以《我们可以成为英雄》回到那个世界。根据官方前提：
    当外星入侵者绑架地球的超级英雄时，他们的孩子被赶到政府的安全屋。但是鞭子聪明的补间小姐莫西·莫雷诺（Yaya Gosselin）会毫不动摇地营救她的超级英雄父亲马库斯·莫雷诺（Petro Pascal）。 Missy与其他超级孩子联手逃脱了他们神秘的政府保姆Granada女士（Priyanka Chopra Jonas）。如果他们要挽救父母，他们将不得不利用自己的力量（从弹性，时间控制到预测未来）共同努力，并组建一支世界末日的团队。
 预告片的开头是Missy的配音，称赞Heroics，成年的超级英雄（像她的父亲Marcus），在不确定性日益增加的时代保护着世界。但是外星人发动了进攻，这是我们的英雄跌倒的日子。捕获了奇迹家伙（Boyd Hollbrook），科技人（克里斯蒂安·斯莱特），马库斯（Marcus）和其他英雄组织，而密西（Missy）则与其他所有超级孩子一起被送到英雄组织总部保护她。他们包括可以控制时间流逝的Rewind（以赛亚·罗素·贝利）和Guppy（Vivien Lyra Blair），这是Sharkboy和Lavagirl的孩子，并继承了两者的力量（她得到了鲨鱼的力量！＆＃34;）。随着外星人接下来可能会来找他们，由米西（Missy）领导的聚集的超级孩子决定联合起来营救他们的父母并拯救地球。
 《我们可以成为英雄》于2020年12月25日在Netflix上首次亮相。它会比其前身更好吗？谁知道？评论家喜欢什么，孩子喜欢什么，通常是不同的。至少不是3D模式。而且我敢肯定，年幼的孩子的父母将很高兴能有一些新鲜的，适合家庭的假期票价，以娱乐他们厌倦大流行的后代。
note_en:
It's been 15 years since the premiere of  The Adventures of Sharkboy and Lavagirl in 3D, Director Richard Rodriguez's fantasy adventure kids' film that was hyped as the movie event of 2005. It fell short of expectations, but Rodriguez clearly retained a deep love for this imaginary world—deep enough that he was keen to revisit it with the forthcoming Netflix standalone sequel,   We Can Be Heroes. And yes, the original Lavagirl, Taylor Dooley, makes an appearance.
  Rodriguez is an impressively versatile director, from his early Western/action films  El Mariachi (1992),  Desperado (1995), and  From Dusk Till Dawn (1996), to the  Spy Kids franchise and last year's science fiction blockbuster,   Alita: Battle Angel. After the success of 2003's S py Kids 3D: Game Over, Rodriguez pitched another immersive kids' film, based on a story by his young son, Racer Max. It featured a young boy named Max, neglected by his parents and bullied at school, who creates an imaginary dream world in his journal, called Planet Drool. The inhabitants include Sharkboy (Taylor Lautner), son of a marine biologist, and Lavagirl (Dooley), who has a tendency to set things on fire—as well as "plughounds" and singing bubbles called LaLas. But when the school bully steals Max's dream journal, those dreams start to bleed into reality.
 The film received mostly negative reviews—it holds a  dismal 19 percent fresh rating on Rotten Tomatoes—largely due to the bad 3D effects and storyline (it  was the brainchild of a seven-year-old, after all). But  The Adventures of Sharkboy and Lavagirl did manage to gross $72 million worldwide (double its budget), the visuals were impressive, and Lautner would go on to star in the  Twilight franchise. (Due to scheduling conflicts, he was unable to appear in  We Can Be Heroes.) Rodriguez also co-wrote a series of children's novels based on the characters. And now he's back in that world with  We Can Be Heroes. Per the official premise:
    When alien invaders kidnap Earth's superheroes, their kids are whisked away to a government safe house. But whip-smart tween Missy Moreno (Yaya Gosselin) will stop at nothing to rescue her superhero dad, Marcus Moreno (Pedro Pascal). Missy teams up with the rest of the superkids to escape their mysterious government babysitter, Ms. Granada (Priyanka Chopra Jonas). If they're going to save their parents, they'll have to work together by using their individual powers — from elasticity to time control to predicting the future — and form an out-of-this-world team.
 The trailer opens with a voiceover by Missy lauding the Heroics, adult superheroes (like her dad, Marcus) who protect the world in an era of increasing uncertainty. But then aliens attack, and "this was the day our heroes fell." Miracle Guy (Boyd Hollbrook), Tech-No (Christian Slater), Marcus, and the rest of the Heroics are captured, and Missy is whisked off to Heroics Headquarters for her protection, along with all the other super kids. They include Rewind (Isaiah Russell-Bailey), who can manipulate the flow of time, and Guppy (Vivien Lyra Blair), who is the child of Sharkboy and Lavagirl and inherited powers from both ("She's got shark strength!"). With the aliens likely coming for them next, the assembled super kids, led by Missy, decide to band together to rescue their parents and save the planet.
 We Can Be Heroes debuts on Netflix on December 25, 2020. Will it be better than its predecessor? Who knows? What critics like, and what children like, are often different. At least it's not in 3D. And I'm sure parents of young children will be grateful to have some fresh, family-friendly holiday fare to amuse their pandemic-weary offspring.
title: 超级孩子必须在《我们可以成为英雄》预告片中与外星人入侵作斗争
title_en: Superkids must battle an alien invasion in We Can Be Heroes trailer

迫使大众进入150亿美元和解协议的科学侦探工作始于少数配备了7万美元的研究人员。
 多年来，西弗吉尼亚大学的研究小组首先发现了大众汽车柴油机排放量的巨大差异，因此一直在争取补助和研究资金以维持生存。它每年150万美元的预算中只有一小部分来自大学，而且正在削减。
 该大学替代燃料发动机和排放中心的主任丹·卡德说：“我仍然睡不着觉，试图弄清楚我将如何在下一个支付周期向这些人付款。”
 卡德（David）的戴维（David）胜过大众歌利亚（Goliath）的成功，说明了汽车制造商与监管部门之间资源的巨大差距。直到最近，使大众汽车面临大量刑事调查和诉讼的道路测试技术从未引起监管机构或汽车制造商的浓厚兴趣或金钱，这仍然不能减轻该组织的财务压力。
  自从去年的骗局曝光以来，现年46岁的Carder先生已取得了通常不属于汽车排放专家的名人程度。 《时代》杂志将他与尼基·米娜（Nicki Minaj），弗拉基米尔·普京（Vladimir Putin）和教皇弗朗西斯（Pope Francis）一起列入了全球100位最有影响力的人物的年度名单。
 但是，在学术界，名声不一定等于财富甚至稳定。
 大众汽车与联邦政府和州政府之间的协议包括47亿美元，用于清洁运输研究和项目，以抵消约50万辆污染程度超过法律允许的大众柴油发动机的排放。 
充其量，卡德先生希望为莫农加黑拉河（Monongahela River）切割的这个丘陵地区的测试中心索取一小笔研究经费。或者，他可以签订合同来验证大众汽车计划对排放量超标的汽车进行维修的有效性。
  他管理的中心在2013年从一个环境组织国际清洁运输委员会获得了7万美元的资助，进行这项研究，最终揭露了大众如何操纵车辆欺骗监管者。研究的最初目的是表明汽车制造商能够满足有害氮氧化物排放的更严格限制。不过，这项工作比预期的要复杂得多，卡德先生不得不从其他渠道弥补2万到3万美元的差额。
 布雷顿先生于1995年在环境保护局担任工程师时，拼凑出便携式排放测量系统的原型。因为他没有该项目的官方预算，所以他借用了他需要的零件或从E.P.A.的设备中改装了这些零件。实验室。
  这项工作有助于揭露作弊案，并且还获得了该机构授权给设备制造商的多项专利。卡德先生称布雷顿先生为移动排放测试的“祖父”。西弗吉尼亚州的研究人员在1999年测试卡车公路排放时，以他的工作为基础。
 尽管取得了这些成功，E.P.A。其他监管机构继续依靠实验室测试来检查汽车排放物。大众汽车利用这种做法，对柴油车进行编程，以在引擎软件检测到汽车在实验室的滚轮上行驶时提高对污染的控制。
 否则，将少量部署大众柴油的排放设备以保护组件或节省燃料。大众在法庭文件中承认，它从未期望过有人在非法软件无法使用的道路上对汽车进行测试。
 困难的部分原因是，测量行驶中的车辆的排放是一项重大的工程挑战。 
Carder先生和他的团队在2013年获得了对汽车进行测试的合同时，就利用了他们对卡车进行测试的经验。面临的挑战之一是在汽车中配备一个移动实验室。当时，可用于此类排放测试的设备仅在短途旅行中才具有足够的电池电量。
 为了使长途旅行成为可能，西弗吉尼亚大学的研究人员在常规的五金店购买了便携式汽油发电机，并将它们用螺栓固定在测试车的后端。这些发电机拍得很厉害，经常摔坏，因为它们的设计目的不是要碰到它们。
 卡德先生及其团队开始测试两辆大众汽车和一辆宝马汽车，事实证明这是合法的。测量设备本身也很挑剔。电线松动，传感器出现故障。
 Marc Besch和Arvind Thiruvengadam，他们的研究生在西海岸进行了试驾，回想起一个深夜，他们在俄勒冈州波特兰市的一家购物中心停车场修理齿轮，在北加州，他们被一名可疑的警官拖了过来。从测试车的后部伸出的奇怪齿轮。
 随着时间的流逝，研究人员注意到，大众捷达和帕萨特这些车辆的排放量比原先的排放量要高得多。但是他们将差异归因于汽车的技术缺陷或设计缺陷，而不是故意的不法行为。
 “这很有趣，但是我们从来没有想过它会给大众汽车带来麻烦。”赫曼思·卡帕纳（Hemanth Kappanna）说，他还以研究生的身份参加了测试，现在在通用汽车工作。 “永远不能。”
 Besch先生在2014年初在圣地亚哥举行的行业会议上介绍了该团队的发现，但仍未意识到他们发现了丑闻。数据激起了加州空气资源委员会官员对听众的兴趣。众所周知，CARB曾与西维吉尼亚小组合作，并让该团队使用州实验室来校准用于大众汽车测试的设备。 
该机构发言人斯坦利·扬说，该机构分配了自己的团队以查明发生了什么。他们测试了一大批大众柴油，检查了汽车的计算机系统，并欺骗了汽车以为他们在路上。
 该机构发现，大众汽车在引擎冷的时候比在暖机的时候减少了污染。通常情况是相反的。
 大众最初否认有任何错误。但是最终，该机构积累了足够的证据，证明公司官员被迫承认这些汽车中装有所谓的失败装置。这家汽车制造商现在在马里兰州，马萨诸塞州和纽约州面临诉讼，声称管理层已意识到这种欺骗行为。
 与西弗吉尼亚大学不同，加利福尼亚保证从其工作中获得回报。尽管CARB不会获得任何资金，但该州将获得大众汽车在用于补偿其造成的环境损害的项目上超过10亿美元的资金。发言人戴夫·克莱格恩（Dave Clegern）表示，该机构将间接受益。
  但是，西弗吉尼亚州的研究人员在E.P.A.正式指控大众汽车在排放测试中作弊，并注意到该大学的作用。
 卡德先生说：“真是太神奇了，有人会故意尝试做这样的事情。”
 消息传出后，他在一个用来测试柴油机排放物的校园实验室里工作，身上沾满了油脂。 
当他的手机开始嗡嗡作响时，他最初的想法是，大学的一个移动排放测试实验室发生了事故。  “我在想，我们的工作人员在哪里？ 有事吗？”
note_en:
MORGANTOWN, W.Va. — The scientific detective work that forced Volkswagen into a  $15 billion settlement began with a handful of researchers armed with just $70,000.
 For years, the research team at West Virginia University, which first noticed big discrepancies in Volkswagen’s diesel emissions, has scrounged for grants and research funding to survive. Only a fraction of its $1.5 million annual budget comes from the university, and that is being cut.
 “I still have sleepless nights trying to figure out how I’m going to pay the guys the next pay cycle,” said Dan Carder, director of the university’s  Center for Alternative Fuels Engines and Emissions.
 The success of Mr. Carder’s David against Volkswagen’s Goliath illustrates the huge disparity in resources between carmakers and oversight groups. The road testing technology that exposed Volkswagen to a raft of  criminal investigations and lawsuits never attracted much interest — or money — from regulators or carmakers until recently, and that is still not certain to relieve the group‘s financial pressure.
  Since the deception came to light last year, Mr. Carder, 46, has achieved a degree of celebrity not usually accorded to vehicle emissions experts. Time magazine named him to its annual list of the world’s 100 most influential people, alongside  Nicki Minaj, Vladimir Putin and Pope Francis.
 But fame does not necessarily equal riches, or even stability, in the world of academia.
 The agreement between Volkswagen and federal and state governments includes $4.7 billion for research into clean transportation and projects to offset the emissions from about 500,000 Volkswagen diesel engines that are polluting more than the law allows.
 At best, Mr. Carder hopes to claim a sliver of that research money for the testing center in this hilly region cut by the Monongahela River. Or he could land contracts to verify the effectiveness of repairs that Volkswagen plans to make on cars with excess emissions.
  The center he runs received $70,000 in 2013 from an environmental group, the  International Council on Clean Transportation, to conduct the study that eventually exposed how Volkswagen had rigged vehicles to deceive regulators. The original purpose of the research was to show that carmakers were capable of meeting more rigorous limits on emissions of harmful nitrogen oxides. The work was more complicated than expected, though, and Mr. Carder had to make up a $20,000 or $30,000 shortfall from other sources.
 While working as an engineer at the Environmental Protection Agency in 1995, Mr. Breton cobbled together a prototype for a portable emissions measurement system. Because he had no official budget for the project, he borrowed parts he needed or adapted them from equipment in E.P.A. labs.
  The work helped expose cheating cases and also led to several patents that the agency licensed to equipment manufacturers. Mr. Carder calls Mr. Breton the “grandfather” of mobile emissions testing. West Virginia researchers built on his work when testing highway emissions for trucks in 1999.
 Despite such successes, the E.P.A. and other regulators have continued to rely on laboratory tests to check auto emissions. Volkswagen took advantage of that practice, programming its diesel vehicles to increase their control of pollution when the engine software detected that the car was running on rollers in a lab.
 Otherwise, emissions equipment in Volkswagen diesels was deployed sparingly to protect components or save fuel. Volkswagen has admitted in court documents that it never expected anyone to test its cars on the road, where the illegal software would not work.
 Part of the difficulty is that measuring the emissions of a moving vehicle is a significant engineering challenge.
 Mr. Carder and his team drew on their experience testing trucks when they got the contract to test cars in 2013. One challenge was to fit what amounts to a mobile laboratory in the car. At the time, the equipment available for such emissions testing had enough battery power only for short trips.
 To make long hauls possible, the West Virginia University researchers bought portable gasoline generators at regular hardware stores and bolted them to the rear ends of the test cars. The generators made a terrible racket and frequently broke down because they were not designed to be bumped around.
 Mr. Carder and his team began testing two Volkswagens and a BMW, which proved to be legal. The measuring equipment itself was finicky, too. Wires came loose, sensors failed.
 Marc Besch and Arvind Thiruvengadam, who as graduate students conducted test drives on the West Coast, recalled a late night spent repairing the gear in a shopping center parking lot in Portland, Ore. In Northern California, they were pulled over by a police officer suspicious of the bizarre gear protruding from the back of the test car.
 Over time, the researchers noticed that emissions from the vehicles — a Volkswagen Jetta and a Passat — were much higher than they should have been. But they attributed the discrepancies to technical defects or design flaws in the cars rather than deliberate wrongdoing.
 “It was interesting, but we never thought it would blow the lid off Volkswagen,” said Hemanth Kappanna, who also took part in the testing as a graduate student and now works at General Motors. “Never, ever.”
 Mr. Besch presented the team’s findings at an industry conference in San Diego in early 2014, still not realizing they had uncovered a scandal. The data piqued the interest of officials in the audience from the California Air Resources Board. CARB, as the agency is known, worked with the West Virginia group before and had let the team use state labs to calibrate equipment for the Volkswagen tests.
 The agency assigned its own team to find out what was going on, said Stanley Young, an agency spokesman. They tested a larger group of Volkswagen diesels, examined the cars’ computer systems and fooled the vehicles into thinking they were on the road.
 The agency found that the Volkswagens emitted less pollution when the engines were cold than when they had warmed up. Normally, it is the other way around.
 Volkswagen initially denied anything was wrong. But eventually, the agency accumulated enough evidence that company officials were forced to confess that the cars contained so-called defeat devices. The carmaker now faces lawsuits in Maryland, Massachusetts and New York claiming that management was aware of the deception.
 Unlike West Virginia University, California is guaranteed a payoff from its work. The state will get more than  $1 billion of the money Volkswagen is spending on projects to compensate for the environmental damage it caused, though CARB will not receive any of that money. The agency will benefit indirectly, Dave Clegern, a spokesman, said.
  The West Virginia researchers did, however, gain recognition when the E.P.A. formally accused Volkswagen of cheating on emissions tests and took note of the university’s role.
 “It’s amazing,” Mr. Carder said, “that someone would purposely try to do something like this.”
 When the news broke, he was covered in grease, working in a campus lab used for testing emissions of diesel engines.
 His initial thought when his mobile phone started buzzing with calls from reporters was that one of the university’s mobile emissions testing labs had been in an accident.
 “I’m thinking, where are our crews at? Did something blow up?”
